q: An infant has an enlarged, cloudy cornea and tearing. The enlarged globe is termed:
w: Raised IOP from congenital glaucoma stretches the infant's distensible cornea and sclera (buphthalmos).
k: Buphthalmos = big infant eye = congenital glaucoma.

q: The most common cause of orbital cellulitis is:
w: The thin lamina papyracea shares the medial orbital wall with the ethmoid, so ethmoiditis is the usual source.

q: The most common primary orbital malignancy in children is:
w: Rhabdomyosarcoma is the most common pediatric primary orbital malignancy (peak 7–8 years).

q: Fatiguable ptosis that improves with a Tensilon (edrophonium) test indicates:
w: Variable, fatiguable ptosis with a positive Tensilon test is myasthenia gravis.

q: The most common type of adult ptosis is:
w: Aponeurotic (levator disinsertion) ptosis is the most common in adults, with a thin lid and high crease.

q: Horner's syndrome triad consists of:
w: Horner's = ptosis + miosis (small pupil) + anhidrosis. CN3 palsy gives the dilated-pupil ptosis.

q: A chronic, non-tender, firm lid nodule is a:
w: A chalazion is a chronic sterile lipogranuloma of the meibomian gland; the hordeolum is the acute, tender abscess.

q: An eyelid that turns outward after a CN VII palsy is:
w: Orbicularis paralysis (CN VII) lets the lid evert (ectropion). Entropion turns in and scratches the cornea.

q: The most common malignant eyelid tumor is:
w: Basal cell carcinoma (pearly margin, lower lid) is the most common eyelid malignancy.

q: The most common malignant lacrimal gland tumor is:
w: Adenoid cystic carcinoma is the most common malignant lacrimal gland tumor (painful, perineural, poor prognosis).
k: Malignant lacrimal tumor = adenoid cystic carcinoma.

q: Chronic dacryocystitis from nasolacrimal duct obstruction is definitively treated with:
w: DCR creates a new drainage passage for chronic obstruction; probing is used for congenital cases.

q: Blood regurgitating on pressure over the lacrimal sac suggests:
w: Pus = infection; blood = a sac tumor (classically SCC).

q: The most common overall cause of proptosis in adults is:
w: Thyroid eye disease (Graves) is the most common cause of both unilateral and bilateral proptosis in adults.

q: Pulsating exophthalmos suggests:
w: A transmitted vascular pulse (pulsating proptosis) suggests a carotid-cavernous fistula; intermittent (Valsalva) suggests varices.

q: Bilateral painless enlargement of the lacrimal AND salivary glands is:
w: Mikulicz syndrome is bilateral painless lacrimal + salivary gland enlargement (sarcoid, lymphoma, TB).

q: Acute dacryoadenitis classically produces what lid sign?
w: Inflammation of the lacrimal gland (upper outer orbit) causes a characteristic S-shaped ptosis.

q: Yellow lipid plaques near the medial canthi (xanthelasma) prompt screening for:
w: Xanthelasma are lipid deposits associated with hyperlipidemia.

q: Inability to fully close the eye (e.g., from facial palsy) is:
w: Lagophthalmos is incomplete lid closure, risking exposure keratopathy.

q: The most common benign orbital tumor in adults is:
w: Cavernous hemangioma is the most common benign orbital tumor in adults.

q: Forceful, involuntary bilateral lid closure treated with botulinum toxin is:
w: Essential blepharospasm is bilateral forced closure managed with botulinum toxin; myokymia is a benign unilateral twitch.

q: An extra row of lashes arising from the meibomian gland orifices is:
w: Distichiasis is an accessory lash row from the meibomian orifices; trichiasis is misdirection of normal lashes.

q: Congenital dacryocystitis in a newborn is most commonly caused by:
w: Congenital (bilateral) dacryocystitis from a non-canalized NLD is classically pneumococcal; probe at 12–18 months if massage fails.

q: A wing-shaped fibrovascular growth crossing the limbus and destroying Bowman's layer is a:
w: A pterygium crosses the limbus and destroys Bowman's; a pinguecula is a yellow limbal deposit that does not cross.

q: The leading infectious cause of blindness worldwide is:
w: Trachoma (Chlamydia trachomatis A, B, C) is the leading infectious cause of world blindness; pannus forms at the upper limbus.

q: A violet ring of injection around the cornea that does not blanch with epinephrine indicates:
w: Ciliary (circumcorneal) flush reflects engorged deep perilimbal vessels seen in serious anterior disease.

q: The earliest sign of keratoconus is:
w: The Fleischer ring (iron at the cone base) is the earliest sign; Munson's sign and hydrops are later.

q: A green-gold ring in Descemet's membrane (Kayser-Fleischer ring) indicates:
w: The KF ring is copper deposition in Descemet's membrane in Wilson's disease.

q: Which growth does NOT cross the limbus?
w: A pinguecula is a degenerative yellow deposit that stops at the limbus.

q: Cobblestone papillae on the upper tarsus with a shield ulcer in a young male is:
w: Vernal KC (seasonal, young males) shows cobblestone papillae, Trantas dots, and a sterile shield ulcer.
k: Vernal KC = cobblestone papillae + shield ulcer.

q: Giant papillary conjunctivitis is most associated with:
w: GPC is caused by lens deposits and friction on the upper tarsus (also sutures, prostheses).

q: In bacterial conjunctivitis, the predominant cells and discharge are:
w: Bacterial conjunctivitis = neutrophils and mucopurulent discharge; viral = lymphocytes/watery; allergic = eosinophils/stringy.

q: A recurrent branching corneal ulcer with terminal bulbs that stains with fluorescein is:
w: The dendritic ulcer is pathognomonic of HSV; topical steroids are contraindicated on epithelial disease.

q: Vesicles on the tip of the nose in herpes zoster ophthalmicus (Hutchinson's sign) indicate:
w: Hutchinson's sign (nasociliary branch of V1) predicts a high risk of ocular involvement.

q: A tonic pupil that constricts to dilute (0.1%) pilocarpine is:
w: Adie's (ciliary ganglion) shows denervation supersensitivity, constricting to 0.1% pilocarpine.

q: Small, irregular pupils that accommodate but do not react to light, due to neurosyphilis, are:
w: Argyll-Robertson pupils (light-near dissociation) indicate neurosyphilis (pretectal lesion).

q: Primary acute angle-closure glaucoma classically shows:
w: PACG presents with a hard painful eye, cloudy cornea, halos, and a mid-dilated fixed pupil; it is a surgical emergency.
k: PACG = mid-dilated fixed pupil, cloudy cornea.

q: A hypermature cataract leaking protein that clogs the trabecular meshwork causes which glaucoma?
w: Phacolytic glaucoma is open-angle (leaked lens protein blocks the meshwork); phacomorphic is the closed-angle swollen-lens type.

q: In Marfan syndrome the crystalline lens dislocates:
w: Marfan (FBN1) dislocates the lens up (superotemporal); homocystinuria dislocates it down (inferonasal).

q: A nuclear sclerotic cataract that temporarily improves near vision causes:
w: Nuclear sclerosis raises the lens index, producing a myopic shift ('second sight') before clouding vision.

q: Which age-related cataract is described as the most common and most visually disabling, and is steroid-linked?
w: Posterior subcapsular cataract disables near vision most and is associated with steroids and diabetes.

q: A snowflake cataract that can progress within 72 hours occurs in:
w: The bilateral snowflake cataract is the classic acute diabetic cataract.

q: Large, greasy 'mutton-fat' keratic precipitates indicate which uveitis?
w: Mutton-fat KP suggest granulomatous uveitis (sarcoid, TB, VKH); fine stellate KP suggest viral or Fuchs.

q: A hypopyon in acute anterior uveitis is most associated with:
w: Hypopyon (pus in the AC) is classic for HLA-B27 uveitis and Behcet's, and for bacterial endophthalmitis.

q: The standard treatment of acute anterior uveitis is:
w: Topical steroids quiet inflammation and a cycloplegic relieves spasm and prevents posterior synechiae.

q: Fuchs heterochromic cyclitis characteristically shows:
w: Fuchs is a chronic unilateral uveitis with a lighter iris, fine KP, and notably no posterior synechiae; causes cataract.

q: Severe, boring eye pain that radiates, often with systemic disease (RA, PTB), describes:
w: Scleritis is deep, severely painful, and linked to systemic disease; episcleritis is superficial and mild.
k: Scleritis = deep boring pain + systemic link.

q: Band keratopathy (calcium in Bowman's layer) is treated with:
w: Band keratopathy is calcium deposition (chronic uveitis, hypercalcemia) removed by EDTA chelation.

q: Aphakia (absence of the lens) results in approximately:
w: Removing the lens leaves a high hyperopic refraction (~+10 to +12 D), corrected by IOL, CL, or thick spectacles.

q: A vertical brown spindle of pigment on the corneal endothelium (Krukenberg spindle) is part of:
w: Krukenberg spindle, iris transillumination defects, and Sampaolesi line make the pigment dispersion triad → pigmentary glaucoma.

q: The dry eye test cutoffs are:
w: Schirmer <10 mm/5 min suggests aqueous deficiency; TBUT <10 sec suggests instability (lipid/mucin).
k: Dry eye: Schirmer <10 mm, TBUT <10 sec.

q: The aqueous deficiency form of dry eye is most classically caused by:
w: Sjogren's is the classic immune cause of aqueous-deficient dry eye; vitamin A and trachoma cause mucin deficiency.

q: After-cataract (posterior capsule opacification) following extracapsular surgery is treated with:
w: YAG laser capsulotomy opens the clouded posterior capsule (after-cataract).

q: Sudden painless monocular vision loss with a pale retina and a cherry-red fovea is:
w: CRAO whitens the inner retina, leaving the choroid-fed fovea visible as a cherry-red spot; rule out giant cell arteritis.

q: A 'blood and thunder' fundus with diffuse hemorrhages and dilated tortuous veins is:
w: Central retinal vein occlusion gives the blood-and-thunder appearance; ischemic CRVO risks neovascular glaucoma at ~3 months.

q: The earliest sign of diabetic retinopathy is:
w: Microaneurysms are the earliest ophthalmoscopic sign of diabetic retinopathy.

q: Severe non-proliferative diabetic retinopathy is defined by the:
w: 4-2-1: hemorrhages in all 4 quadrants, OR venous beading in 2, OR IRMA in 1.

q: Cotton-wool spots represent:
w: Cotton-wool spots are NFL microinfarcts from ischemia; hard exudates are lipid.

q: Proliferative diabetic retinopathy is treated with:
w: PRP destroys ischemic retina to cut the VEGF drive; anti-VEGF is an adjunct.

q: A macular star of hard exudates with papilledema indicates:
w: Grade IV (Keith-Wagener-Barker) hypertensive retinopathy adds papilledema to grade III findings = malignant hypertension.

q: The most common type of age-related macular degeneration is:
w: Dry (atrophic) ARMD with drusen is 85–90%; wet (CNV) is the aggressive minority.

q: First-line treatment for wet (neovascular) ARMD is:
w: Anti-VEGF (ranibizumab, bevacizumab, aflibercept) is first-line for choroidal neovascularization.

q: Night blindness, bone-spicule pigmentation, and a waxy pale disc describe:
w: RP is a rod-first dystrophy: nyctalopia, bone spicules, attenuated vessels, waxy disc, reduced rod ERG.

q: A 'headlight in the fog' focal chorioretinitis at the edge of an old scar is:
w: Toxoplasmosis (the most common posterior uveitis) reactivates at an old pigmented scar with overlying vitritis.

q: A 'pizza pie' retinitis with hemorrhage in a patient with HIV (CD4 <50) is:
w: CMV retinitis (immunocompromised) shows the pizza-pie / brushfire appearance; treat with ganciclovir/foscarnet.

q: Which retinal detachment type has an actual retinal break?
w: Only rhegmatogenous RD has a break (e.g., horseshoe tear). Tractional and exudative have no break.

q: Pigment cells in the anterior vitreous (tobacco dust / Shafer's sign) indicate:
w: Tobacco dust signals a retinal break and warrants urgent retinal examination.

q: Unilateral vision loss over days with pain on eye movement and an RAPD in a young woman is:
w: Optic neuritis (often the first sign of multiple sclerosis) causes painful unilateral loss with RAPD and red desaturation.
k: Painful unilateral loss + RAPD = optic neuritis (MS).

q: Papilledema differs from optic neuritis in that papilledema is:
w: Papilledema is bilateral disc swelling from raised ICP, with vision spared early; optic neuritis is unilateral and painful.

q: A 72-year-old with jaw claudication, raised ESR, and a chalky-white swollen disc has:
w: Arteritic AION from giant cell arteritis is an emergency; start high-dose steroids before the biopsy to save the other eye.

q: Painless vision loss on waking with an altitudinal field defect and a 'disc at risk' is:
w: NAION (nocturnal hypotension, small crowded disc, vascular risk factors) gives a painless altitudinal defect.
k: NAION = altitudinal defect, disc at risk, on waking.

q: Panuveitis with vitiligo, poliosis, alopecia, and meningismus is:
w: VKH is a panuveitis with integumentary and neurological features, treated with high-dose systemic steroids.

q: After penetrating trauma to one eye, bilateral granulomatous panuveitis develops. The injured eye is called the:
w: In sympathetic ophthalmia the injured eye is the 'exciting' eye and the fellow is the 'sympathizing' eye; prevent by enucleation within 10–14 days.

q: Retinopathy of prematurity active disease ('plus disease') is marked by:
w: Plus disease (vascular dilation + tortuosity) marks active ROP; stage 5 is total RD with leukocoria.

q: A branch retinal vein occlusion most commonly occurs:
w: BRVO occurs where a thickened artery compresses a vein at a crossing, most often superotemporally.

q: Which retinal break carries the highest risk of progressing to detachment?
w: A horseshoe (flap) tear is under active vitreoretinal traction and most likely to lead to RD.

q: Leukocoria in a child with a peripheral granuloma from dog/cat roundworm suggests:
w: Ocular toxocariasis (Toxocara) causes a granuloma and leukocoria in children.

q: On the optic disc, glaucomatous cupping violates which rule?
w: The healthy neuroretinal rim follows Inferior>Superior>Nasal>Temporal (ISNT); glaucoma violates it (often inferior notching).

q: A large solitary floater after acute posterior vitreous detachment (a ring of condensed peripapillary vitreous) is a:
w: The Weiss ring is detached peripapillary vitreous seen in PVD; new floaters/flashes warrant a retinal exam for breaks.

q: Distortion of straight lines (metamorphopsia) on an Amsler grid is an early sign of:
w: Metamorphopsia signals macular pathology, classically early CNV in wet ARMD.

q: A worker splashes lye (alkali) in one eye. The correct first step is:
w: A chemical burn is the only ocular injury treated before history and exam; irrigate at once.
k: Chemical burn = irrigate first, history later.

q: Compared with acid, an alkali ocular burn is:
w: Alkali causes liquefactive necrosis and drives deeper; acid coagulates surface proteins and self-limits.

q: Chemical-burn irrigation should continue until the pH reaches:
w: Irrigate copiously and continue until the conjunctival pH normalizes to about 7.0–7.4.

q: The prognosis in a chemical burn (Roper-Hall) depends most on:
w: Limbal (stem cell) ischemia is the key prognostic factor in the Roper-Hall grading.

q: A hard, painful red eye with a mid-dilated fixed pupil and cloudy cornea is managed by:
w: Acute angle-closure is an emergency: lower IOP with drops/oral agents, then definitive laser iridotomy.
k: Acute angle-closure = lower IOP then iridotomy.

q: The treatment window to attempt to save vision in CRAO is about:
w: Within ~4–6 hours, ocular massage and IOP lowering may help; always exclude giant cell arteritis.

q: In suspected giant cell arteritis with sudden vision loss, you should:
o: Wait for temporal artery biopsy before treating
w: Treat immediately to protect the second eye; the biopsy can follow within days.

q: A suspected open (ruptured) globe should be:
w: Place a rigid shield (not a pad), avoid all pressure and IOP measurement, keep NPO, and arrange urgent surgery.
k: Open globe = shield, no pressure, NPO.

q: Which imaging is CONTRAINDICATED for a suspected ferrous (iron) intraocular foreign body?
w: MRI is contraindicated with a ferrous IOFB (magnetic movement risk); use CT.

q: An iron intraocular foreign body causes which toxic syndrome?
w: Iron causes siderosis (rusty iris, heterochromia, b-wave attenuation); copper alloy causes chalcosis (KF ring, sunflower cataract).
k: Iron IOFB = siderosis; copper alloy = chalcosis.

q: A hypopyon and decreasing vision a few days after cataract surgery is:
w: Post-operative endophthalmitis is an emergency treated with a vitreous tap and intravitreal antibiotics.

q: Which finding distinguishes ORBITAL from preseptal cellulitis?
w: Orbital cellulitis adds proptosis, pain on motion, and restricted gaze; preseptal has normal motility and no proptosis.

q: A blow-out orbital floor fracture classically presents with:
w: Floor fracture causes infraorbital nerve numbness, restricted up/down gaze, enophthalmos; best imaged with CT coronal sections.

q: After a blunt orbital injury with a floor fracture, the patient should be told to:
o: Blow the nose to clear pressure
w: Nose-blowing can force air into the orbit (emphysema) and worsen the injury; avoid it.

q: A traumatic hyphema carries the greatest risk of rebleeding on which days?
w: Hyphema rebleeding peaks days 3–5 and can spike IOP; rest, shield, avoid antiplatelets, and screen for sickle cell.

q: Bilateral retinal hemorrhages with subdural hemorrhage in an infant under 2 suggest:
w: Bilateral retinal hemorrhages plus intracranial bleeding in a young child is shaken baby syndrome; mandatory reporting.
k: Bilateral retinal hemorrhages + subdural = shaken baby.

q: A white corneal infiltrate with fluorescein staining and pain in a contact-lens wearer is, until proven otherwise:
w: An infiltrate with staining and pain is microbial keratitis (Pseudomonas most common), a sight-threatening emergency.

q: UV photokeratitis ('welder's flash' or snow blindness) typically:
w: UV photokeratitis is intensely painful but self-limiting, healing in 24–48 hours (cool compress, NSAID, lubrication).

q: Which acids behave more like alkali, penetrating deeply?
w: Hydrofluoric acid (and ammonia among alkalis) penetrates deeply, unlike most acids that coagulate and self-limit.

q: A pigment ring imprinted on the anterior lens after blunt trauma (from the iris) is a:
w: A Vossius ring is iris-pigment imprinted on the anterior lens capsule from blunt trauma.
k: Vossius ring = blunt trauma lens pigment imprint.

q: Gray retinal opacification after blunt trauma that usually resolves in weeks is:
w: Commotio retinae (Berlin's edema) is transient gray retinal whitening that typically resolves over 2–6 weeks.

q: An abnormally enlarged globe in an infant is termed:
w: Buphthalmos is the ox-eye enlargement of congenital glaucoma: the infant sclera is elastic, so raised IOP stretches the whole globe rather than only cupping the disc.

q: The most common primary orbital malignancy of childhood is:
w: Rhabdomyosarcoma is the commonest primary orbital malignancy in children, classically rapid-onset unilateral proptosis. Retinoblastoma is intraocular, not orbital.

q: Variable, fatiguable ptosis that improves with a Tensilon test indicates:
w: Myasthenia produces fatiguable ptosis that worsens through the day and improves with edrophonium. It is the great mimic of pupil-sparing motility disorders.

q: The most common cause of acquired ptosis in adults is:
w: Aponeurotic ptosis from levator aponeurosis dehiscence is the commonest adult cause, giving a high lid crease with preserved levator function.

q: A non-tender, firm lid granuloma is a:
w: A chalazion is a sterile lipogranuloma of a blocked meibomian gland: firm and non-tender. A hordeolum (stye) is an acute, tender abscess.

q: Ectropion caused by a nerve palsy points to:
w: CN7 palsy paralyzes orbicularis oculi, so the lower lid falls away from the globe, producing paralytic ectropion with exposure keratopathy.

q: The most common eyelid malignancy is:
w: BCC accounts for the great majority of lid malignancies, favors the lower lid and medial canthus, and classically shows a pearly rolled border with madarosis.

q: The most common malignant tumor of the lacrimal gland is:
w: Adenoid cystic carcinoma is the commonest lacrimal gland malignancy, notable for pain from perineural invasion. Pleomorphic adenoma is the commonest benign tumor.

q: The definitive surgical treatment for chronic dacryocystitis is:
w: DCR bypasses the obstructed nasolacrimal duct by creating a direct passage from sac to nasal cavity.

q: Which growth invades and destroys Bowman's membrane?
w: A pterygium crosses the limbus onto the cornea and destroys Bowman's, which is why it can scar and recur. A pinguecula stops at the limbus.
k: Pterygium crosses the limbus, destroys Bowman's.

q: The world's leading infectious cause of blindness is:
w: Trachoma, from Chlamydia trachomatis, leads infectious blindness worldwide through repeated infection, scarring, trichiasis and corneal opacification.

q: The earliest clinical sign of keratoconus is:
w: The Fleischer ring, iron deposited at the base of the cone, appears earliest. Vogt's striae and Munson's sign come later.

q: A copper ring deposited in Descemet's membrane is called a:
w: The KF ring is copper in Descemet's and signals Wilson's disease. The Fleischer ring is iron, at the cone base in keratoconus.

q: Which treatment must NEVER be given alone in epithelial (dendritic) herpes simplex keratitis?
w: Steroids suppress local immunity and let the dendrite enlarge into a geographic ulcer, risking perforation. Steroids have a role only in stromal disease, always under antiviral cover.
k: Never steroids alone on epithelial herpes.

q: In Marfan syndrome the crystalline lens classically dislocates:
w: Marfan displaces the lens up and out; homocystinuria displaces it down and in. Remember Marfan = up, homocystinuria = down.

q: Which cataract type produces 'second sight'?
w: A sclerosing nucleus raises the lens index, producing an index myopia that briefly lets a presbyope read without glasses again.

q: A hypermature cataract leaking lens protein through an intact capsule causes which OPEN-angle secondary glaucoma?
w: In phacolytic glaucoma leaked protein and macrophages clog an open trabecular meshwork. Phacomorphic glaucoma is the angle-closure form, from an intumescent lens.

q: Large, greasy 'mutton-fat' keratic precipitates indicate which type of uveitis?
w: Mutton-fat KP are large macrophage-laden deposits marking granulomatous uveitis (sarcoid, TB, syphilis, sympathetic ophthalmia). Fine KP suggest non-granulomatous disease.

q: A 'blood-and-thunder' fundus describes:
w: Central retinal vein occlusion floods all four quadrants with flame hemorrhages, dilated tortuous veins and disc edema, the blood-and-thunder appearance.

q: The earliest visible sign of diabetic retinopathy is:
w: Microaneurysms are the first ophthalmoscopic sign, outpouchings where pericyte loss has weakened the capillary wall.

q: The 4-2-1 rule is used to define:
w: Severe NPDR needs any one of: hemorrhages in 4 quadrants, venous beading in 2, or IRMA in 1. It flags a high risk of progressing to proliferative disease.

q: The most common form of age-related macular degeneration is:
w: Dry AMD accounts for roughly 90% of cases and progresses slowly through drusen to geographic atrophy. Wet AMD is less common but causes most severe central loss.
k: Dry AMD commonest (~90%); wet causes most severe loss.

q: First-line treatment for wet AMD is:
w: Anti-VEGF injections are first-line for choroidal neovascularization. AREDS2 slows progression in intermediate dry AMD but does not treat wet disease.

q: A 'headlight in the fog' fundus appearance is classic for:
w: An active toxoplasma focus glows through overlying vitritis beside an old pigmented scar, the headlight-in-the-fog picture.

q: Jaw claudication with a chalky-white swollen disc indicates:
w: Arteritic AION from GCA gives a chalky, pallid disc with systemic symptoms and a raised ESR. High-dose steroids start immediately, before biopsy, to save the fellow eye.
k: Jaw claudication + chalky disc = GCA, steroids now.

q: A retinal detachment associated with a full-thickness retinal break is:
w: Rhegma means rent: fluid passes through a break into the subretinal space. Tractional detachment pulls the retina up without a break; exudative accumulates fluid beneath it.

q: For a chemical burn, the very first action is to:
w: Irrigation precedes everything, including history and acuity. Every second of contact deepens the injury, and alkali penetrates fastest.
k: Chemical burn: irrigate before anything else.

q: Initial management of a central retinal artery occlusion includes:
w: Ocular massage and IOP-lowering aim to dislodge the embolus distally. Giant cell arteritis must be excluded at once, since it threatens the fellow eye.
k: CRAO: massage, lower IOP, exclude GCA.

q: In suspected giant cell arteritis, high-dose steroids should be started:
o: Only if vision is already lost
w: Steroids start on clinical suspicion. Biopsy stays informative for one to two weeks afterward, and waiting risks blinding the fellow eye.

q: A suspected ferrous intraocular foreign body must be imaged with:
w: CT is mandatory and MRI is contraindicated: the magnet can move a ferrous fragment and shred intraocular tissue.

q: A hypopyon appearing days after cataract surgery indicates:
w: Post-operative endophthalmitis presents with pain, falling vision and a hypopyon, and demands urgent intravitreal antibiotics.

q: Orbital cellulitis is managed with:
o: Oral antibiotics and review in a week
w: Orbital cellulitis needs admission, IV antibiotics and CT to look for an abscess. It can progress to cavernous sinus thrombosis and death.

q: A patient with a blow-out fracture should specifically be told not to:
w: Nose blowing forces air from the sinus into the orbit through the fracture, producing orbital emphysema and potentially compressing the optic nerve.
k: Blow-out fracture: do not blow the nose.

q: A positive Seidel test with a distorted pupil suggests:
w: Seidel positivity is aqueous streaming through a wound. With a peaked pupil it means an open globe: shield the eye, keep the patient NPO, apply no pressure, and refer for surgery.

q: The cataract described as the most visually disabling age-related type is:
w: PSC sits right at the nodal point of the visual axis, so it wrecks near vision and glares badly in bright light, out of all proportion to its size.
k: PSC = most visually disabling age-related cataract.

q: The Kayser-Fleischer ring consists of:
w: The KF ring is copper deposited in Descemet's, signalling Wilson's disease. The Fleischer ring is iron at the base of the keratoconus cone: different metal, different layer, different disease.

q: The cherry-red spot in CRAO represents:
o: Healthy choroid seen through thin retina
w: The spot is not diseased tissue. The fovea is thin and fed by the choroid, so its normal red glow shows through while the surrounding retina turns pale and ischemic. Tay-Sachs produces the same picture.
k: Cherry-red spot = healthy choroid showing through.

q: Compared with optic neuritis, papilledema early on shows:
w: Papilledema spares acuity early, showing only an enlarged blind spot, which is why raised ICP can go unnoticed. Optic neuritis loses vision early and painfully with an RAPD.
k: Papilledema spares vision early; neuritis does not.

q: Which retinal detachment type has NO retinal break?
w: Only rhegmatogenous involves a break, the rhegma. Tractional detachment pulls the retina up with fibrovascular membranes; exudative accumulates fluid beneath it. Neither tears the retina.

q: Alkali burns are more dangerous than acid burns because alkali:
w: Alkali saponifies membranes and liquefies its way inward, so damage continues long after contact. Acid coagulates protein, and that coagulum forms a barrier that limits depth. Acid is self-limiting; alkali is not.
k: Alkali liquefies and keeps going; acid coagulates and stops.

q: The pupil in acute angle-closure glaucoma is:
w: The mid-dilated pupil is the worst position: maximal iris-lens contact gives pupillary block while the peripheral iris bunches into the angle. A pinpoint pupil points elsewhere.

q: A chemical burn is unique among ocular injuries because it is:
o: Treated before any history is taken
w: It is the only injury where treatment precedes history and even acuity. Every second of contact deepens the burn, so irrigation starts the moment the patient arrives.
k: Chemical burn: the only injury treated before history.

q: A painful, tender, red nodule erupts at the lid margin over a few days. Most likely:
w: Acute, tender bacterial abscess. A chalazion is chronic and non-tender.

q: A patient with Bell's palsy (CN VII) develops a lower lid that falls away from the globe. This is:
w: (paralytic). Orbicularis paralysis lets the lid evert. Lagophthalmos (incomplete closure) often coexists, but the everted lid is ectropion.

q: Fluorescein placed in the eye is not recovered from the nose; after saline irrigation the dye is recovered. This indicates:
w: A negative Jones I with dye recovered on Jones II irrigation indicates partial (functional) obstruction proximal to the sac.
k: A negative Jones I with dye recovered on Jones II irrigation indicates partial (functional) obstruction…

q: Chronic dacryocystitis with blood regurgitating on pressure over the sac should raise suspicion for:
o: Squamous cell carcinoma of the sac
w: Pus suggests infection; blood suggests a sac tumor, classically squamous cell carcinoma.
k: Pus suggests infection; blood suggests a sac tumor, classically squamous cell carcinoma.

q: A red eye has a violet ring of injection around the cornea that does not blanch with epinephrine. This points to:
w: Ciliary (circumcorneal) flush reflects engorged deep perilimbal vessels, seen in uveitis, keratitis, or acute angle-closure. Surface conditions give bright, mobile conjunctival injection that blanches.
k: Ciliary (circumcorneal) flush reflects engorged deep perilimbal vessels, seen in uveitis, keratitis, or acute…

q: A green-gold ring is found in Descemet's membrane. The systemic disease is:
w: The Kayser-Fleischer ring is copper deposition in Descemet's membrane in hepatolenticular degeneration.
k: The Kayser-Fleischer ring is copper deposition in Descemet's membrane in hepatolenticular degeneration.

q: A tall patient with long fingers and an aortic aneurysm has a lens dislocated superotemporally. The diagnosis and shift are:
w: Marfan dislocates the lens superotemporally (up); homocystinuria dislocates it inferonasally (down).
k: Marfan dislocates the lens superotemporally (up); homocystinuria dislocates it inferonasally (down).

q: A hypermature cataract leaks lens protein, macrophages clog the trabecular meshwork, and IOP rises with an open angle. This is:
w: (open angle). Phacomorphic is the closed-angle type from a swollen intumescent lens.

q: Mutton-fat keratic precipitates on the corneal endothelium indicate which kind of uveitis?
w: Large greasy mutton-fat KP suggest sarcoidosis, TB, VKH. Fine stellate KP suggest viral or Fuchs.
k: Large greasy mutton-fat KP suggest sarcoidosis, TB, VKH.

q: Sudden painless loss of vision in one eye shows a pale retina with a cherry-red fovea and an RAPD. The diagnosis and first step:
w: The pale retina with cherry-red fovea is classic. Attempt ocular massage and IOP lowering within the window, and exclude giant cell arteritis.
k: The pale retina with cherry-red fovea is classic.

q: A diabetic has hemorrhages in all four quadrants and venous beading in two quadrants, but no new vessels. This is:
w: The 4-2-1 rule is met (4-quadrant hemorrhage and 2-quadrant venous beading). New vessels would make it proliferative.
k: The 4-2-1 rule is met (4-quadrant hemorrhage and 2-quadrant venous beading).

q: A 30-year-old woman has unilateral vision loss over days, pain on eye movement, red desaturation, and an RAPD. The key systemic association is:
w: This is optic neuritis, the first presentation of MS in about 25% of patients. GCA affects older patients and is painless arteritic ischemia.
k: This is optic neuritis, the first presentation of MS in about 25% of patients.

q: A 72-year-old man has sudden vision loss, jaw pain when chewing, scalp tenderness, and ESR of 80. The immediate action is:
w: Giant cell arteritis can blind the second eye within days; treat on suspicion and biopsy afterward.
k: Giant cell arteritis can blind the second eye within days; treat on suspicion and biopsy afterward.

q: Night blindness, bone-spicule pigmentation, and a waxy pale disc with an extinguished rod ERG describe:
w: Rod-first dystrophy: nyctalopia, bone spicules, attenuated vessels, waxy disc, reduced rod b-wave.
k: Rod-first dystrophy: nyctalopia, bone spicules, attenuated vessels, waxy disc, reduced rod b-wave.

q: A painful red eye has a cloudy cornea, a mid-dilated non-reactive pupil, and a rock-hard globe with nausea. The diagnosis and definitive treatment:
w: Lower the IOP medically first, then perform a definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.
k: Lower the IOP medically first, then perform a definitive laser peripheral iridotomy.

q: A metal worker has a suspected intraocular foreign body of iron. Which is contraindicated?
w: Is contraindicated with a ferrous foreign body (magnetic movement risk). Use CT.
k: Is contraindicated with a ferrous foreign body (magnetic movement risk).

q: Three days after blunt trauma, a patient with hyphema suddenly worsens. The most likely event is:
w: Hyphema rebleeding peaks days 3 to 5 and raises IOP. Manage with rest, shield, and avoidance of antiplatelet drugs; screen for sickle cell.
k: Hyphema rebleeding peaks days 3 to 5 and raises IOP.

q: A blunt orbital injury causes numbness of the cheek and upper lip, diplopia on upgaze, and orbital emphysema. The injury and imaging of choice:
w: Infraorbital nerve numbness, restricted vertical gaze, and emphysema are classic. CT with coronal sections is best, and the patient must not blow the nose.
k: Infraorbital nerve numbness, restricted vertical gaze, and emphysema are classic.

q: Which term describes anterior globe protrusion specifically; Graves disease is the #1 cause?
w: Exophthalmos: ex = out + ophthalmos = eye. Anterior globe protrusion specifically; Graves disease is the #1 cause
k: Exophthalmos = Anterior globe protrusion specifically; Graves disease is the #1 cause

q: Which term describes lid margin turns inward; lashes abrade the cornea?
w: Entropion: en = in + trope = turning. Lid margin turns inward; lashes abrade the cornea
k: Entropion = Lid margin turns inward; lashes abrade the cornea

q: Which term describes lid margin turns outward; causes exposure and epiphora?
w: Ectropion: ec = out + trope = turning. Lid margin turns outward; causes exposure and epiphora
k: Ectropion = Lid margin turns outward; causes exposure and epiphora

q: Which term describes incomplete lid closure ("hare's eye"); leads to exposure keratopathy?
w: Lagophthalmos: Greek lagos = hare + ophthalmos. Incomplete lid closure ("hare's eye"); leads to exposure keratopathy
k: Lagophthalmos = Incomplete lid closure ("hare's eye"); leads to exposure keratopathy

q: Which term describes acute, tender lid-margin abscess (stye)?
w: Hordeolum: Latin hordeum = barley. Acute, tender lid-margin abscess (stye)

q: Which term describes loss of lashes/brows, classic for ulcerative blepharitis?
w: Madarosis: Greek madaros = bald. Loss of lashes/brows, classic for ulcerative blepharitis
k: Madarosis = Loss of lashes/brows, classic for ulcerative blepharitis

q: Which term describes tear overflow from drainage block or reflex tearing?
w: Epiphora: Greek = sudden flow of tears. Tear overflow from drainage block or reflex tearing
k: Epiphora = Tear overflow from drainage block or reflex tearing

q: Which term describes lacrimal gland inflammation; S-shaped ptosis?
w: Dacryoadenitis: dakryon = tear + aden = gland. Lacrimal gland inflammation; S-shaped ptosis
k: Dacryoadenitis = Lacrimal gland inflammation; S-shaped ptosis

q: Which term describes lacrimal sac infection; medial canthal swelling?
w: Dacryocystitis: dakryon + kystis = sac. Lacrimal sac infection; medial canthal swelling
k: Dacryocystitis = Lacrimal sac infection; medial canthal swelling

q: Which term describes accessory second row of eyelashes?
w: Distichiasis: di = two + stichos = row. Accessory second row of eyelashes
k: Distichiasis = Accessory second row of eyelashes

q: Which term describes adhesion between palpebral and bulbar conjunctiva?
w: Symblepharon: syn = together + blepharon = lid. Adhesion between palpebral and bulbar conjunctiva
k: Symblepharon = Adhesion between palpebral and bulbar conjunctiva

q: Which term describes yellow degenerative limbal deposit; does not cross the limbus?
w: Pinguecula: Latin pinguis = fat. Yellow degenerative limbal deposit; does not cross the limbus
k: Pinguecula = Yellow degenerative limbal deposit; does not cross the limbus

q: Which term describes deep violet circumcorneal injection = keratitis/uveitis/acute glaucoma?
w: Ciliary flush: perilimbal "flush". Deep violet circumcorneal injection = keratitis/uveitis/acute glaucoma
k: Ciliary flush = Deep violet circumcorneal injection = keratitis/uveitis/acute glaucoma

q: Which term describes layered white cells in the anterior chamber?
w: Hypopyon: hypo = under + pyon = pus. Layered white cells in the anterior chamber
k: Hypopyon = Layered white cells in the anterior chamber

q: Which term describes mutton-fat = granulomatous; fine stellate = viral/Fuchs?
w: Keratic precipitates: inflammatory cells on endothelium. Mutton-fat = granulomatous; fine stellate = viral/Fuchs
k: Keratic precipitates = Mutton-fat = granulomatous; fine stellate = viral/Fuchs

q: Which term describes iris adhesions: anterior (to cornea) or posterior (to lens)?
w: Synechiae: Greek synecheia = continuity. Iris adhesions: anterior (to cornea) or posterior (to lens)
k: Synechiae = Iris adhesions: anterior (to cornea) or posterior (to lens)

q: Which term describes iris neovascularization; closed angle without pupillary block?
w: Rubeosis iridis: Latin rubeo = redden. Iris neovascularization; closed angle without pupillary block
k: Rubeosis iridis = Iris neovascularization; closed angle without pupillary block

q: Which term describes lens dislocation; Marfan up, homocystinuria down?
w: Ectopia lentis: ec = out + topos = place. Lens dislocation; Marfan up, homocystinuria down
k: Ectopia lentis = Lens dislocation; Marfan up, homocystinuria down

q: Which term describes open-angle glaucoma from leaked hypermature lens protein?
w: Phacolytic: phakos = lens + lysis = loosening. Open-angle glaucoma from leaked hypermature lens protein
k: Phacolytic = Open-angle glaucoma from leaked hypermature lens protein

q: Which term describes closed-angle glaucoma from an intumescent swollen lens?
w: Phacomorphic: phakos + morphe = shape. Closed-angle glaucoma from an intumescent swollen lens
k: Phacomorphic = Closed-angle glaucoma from an intumescent swollen lens

q: Which term describes peripheral stromal cholesterol ring with a lucid interval?
w: Arcus senilis: Latin = bow of old age. Peripheral stromal cholesterol ring with a lucid interval
k: Arcus senilis = Peripheral stromal cholesterol ring with a lucid interval

q: Which term describes pigment in anterior vitreous = retinal break until proven otherwise?
w: Shafer's sign: "tobacco dust". Pigment in anterior vitreous = retinal break until proven otherwise
k: Shafer's sign = Pigment in anterior vitreous = retinal break until proven otherwise

q: Which term describes retinal detachment caused by a full-thickness break?
w: Rhegmatogenous: Greek rhegma = break/rent. Retinal detachment caused by a full-thickness break
k: Rhegmatogenous = Retinal detachment caused by a full-thickness break

q: Which term describes earliest visible sign of diabetic retinopathy?
w: Microaneurysm: micro + aneurysma = dilation. Earliest visible sign of diabetic retinopathy
k: Microaneurysm = Earliest visible sign of diabetic retinopathy

q: Which term describes ischemic swelling of axons, not an exudate?
w: Cotton-wool spot: nerve fiber layer infarct. Ischemic swelling of axons, not an exudate
k: Cotton-wool spot = Ischemic swelling of axons, not an exudate

q: Which term describes shunt vessels in ischemic retina; severe NPDR marker?
w: IRMA: intraretinal microvascular abnormality. Shunt vessels in ischemic retina; severe NPDR marker
k: IRMA = Shunt vessels in ischemic retina; severe NPDR marker

q: Which term describes sub-RPE deposits; hallmark of dry ARMD?
w: Drusen: German = nodule. Sub-RPE deposits; hallmark of dry ARMD
k: Drusen = Sub-RPE deposits; hallmark of dry ARMD

q: Which term describes wavy distortion on Amsler grid; early CNV warning?
w: Metamorphopsia: meta = change + morphe = shape. Wavy distortion on Amsler grid; early CNV warning
k: Metamorphopsia = Wavy distortion on Amsler grid; early CNV warning

q: Which term describes night blindness; first symptom of retinitis pigmentosa?
w: Nyctalopia: Greek nyx = night + alaos = blind. Night blindness; first symptom of retinitis pigmentosa
k: Nyctalopia = Night blindness; first symptom of retinitis pigmentosa

q: Which term describes classic mid-peripheral sign of retinitis pigmentosa?
w: Bone-spicule pigment: perivascular pigment migration. Classic mid-peripheral sign of retinitis pigmentosa
k: Bone-spicule pigment = Classic mid-peripheral sign of retinitis pigmentosa

q: Which term describes bilateral disc swelling from raised intracranial pressure?
w: Papilledema: papilla + oidema = swelling. Bilateral disc swelling from raised intracranial pressure
k: Papilledema = Bilateral disc swelling from raised intracranial pressure

q: Which term describes marcus Gunn pupil; optic nerve or massive retinal disease?
w: RAPD: relative afferent pupillary defect. Marcus Gunn pupil; optic nerve or massive retinal disease
k: RAPD = Marcus Gunn pupil; optic nerve or massive retinal disease

q: Which term describes acid burn; the coagulum self-limits depth?
w: Coagulative necrosis: proteins coagulate. Acid burn; the coagulum self-limits depth
k: Coagulative necrosis = Acid burn; the coagulum self-limits depth

q: Which term describes positive = aqueous leak from a full-thickness corneal wound?
w: Seidel test: fluorescein streaming. Positive = aqueous leak from a full-thickness corneal wound
k: Seidel test = Positive = aqueous leak from a full-thickness corneal wound

q: Which term describes shield, no pressure, NPO, urgent surgery?
w: Open globe: full-thickness scleral/corneal breach. Shield, no pressure, NPO, urgent surgery
k: Open globe = Shield, no pressure, NPO, urgent surgery

q: Which term describes blood in the anterior chamber after blunt trauma?
w: Hyphema: Greek hypo + haima = blood. Blood in the anterior chamber after blunt trauma
k: Hyphema = Blood in the anterior chamber after blunt trauma

q: Which term describes layered white cells; endophthalmitis until proven otherwise?
w: Hypopyon: hypo + pyon = pus. Layered white cells; endophthalmitis until proven otherwise
k: Hypopyon = Layered white cells; endophthalmitis until proven otherwise

q: Which term describes iron IOFB toxicity; no MRI (ferrous)?
w: Siderosis: Greek sideros = iron. Iron IOFB toxicity; no MRI (ferrous)
k: Siderosis = Iron IOFB toxicity; no MRI (ferrous)

q: Which term describes copper IOFB; KF ring + sunflower cataract?
w: Chalcosis: Greek chalkos = copper. Copper IOFB; KF ring + sunflower cataract
k: Chalcosis = Copper IOFB; KF ring + sunflower cataract

q: Which term describes sunken globe; blow-out floor fracture?
w: Enophthalmos: en = in + ophthalmos. Sunken globe; blow-out floor fracture
k: Enophthalmos = Sunken globe; blow-out floor fracture

q: Which term describes gray retinal opacification after blunt trauma; usually resolves?
w: Commotio retinae: Latin = shaking of retina. Gray retinal opacification after blunt trauma; usually resolves
k: Commotio retinae = Gray retinal opacification after blunt trauma; usually resolves

q: Which term describes ring of iris pigment on the lens after blunt trauma?
w: Vossius ring: pigment imprint. Ring of iris pigment on the lens after blunt trauma
k: Vossius ring = Ring of iris pigment on the lens after blunt trauma

q: A positive dark-room provocative test for angle-closure is an IOP rise of more than:
w: The dark-room or prone provocative test is positive at a rise above 8 mmHg. The water-drinking test for POAG uses a different threshold: above 5 mmHg.
k: Provocative test PACG >8 mmHg; WDT POAG >5 mmHg.

q: The water drinking test is performed by having a fasting patient drink:
w: One liter in under five minutes on an empty stomach; a rise above 5 mmHg is positive for POAG. The rapid load transiently raises episcleral venous pressure and unmasks poor outflow.
k: WDT: 1 L in <5 min; positive if IOP rises >5 mmHg.

q: A central corneal thickness below which value marks a POAG risk factor?
w: A CCT under 520 microns is an independent POAG risk factor and also makes Goldmann under-read the true IOP, so a thin cornea is doubly dangerous.
k: CCT <520 µm = POAG risk (and falsely low IOP).

q: An asymmetry in cup-to-disc ratio between the two eyes is suspicious when it exceeds:
w: Asymmetry above 0.2 between fellow eyes is suspicious even when both ratios look individually acceptable, because glaucoma is rarely perfectly symmetric. An absolute C/D above 0.6 is separately suspicious.

q: Which oral osmotic agent is SAFE in a diabetic patient?
w: Isosorbide is the oral osmotic of choice in diabetes. Glycerol is metabolized to glucose and raises blood sugar, so it is contraindicated. Mannitol and urea are IV only, and urea is contraindicated in renal disease.
k: Diabetic oral osmotic = isosorbide, never glycerol.

q: Mannitol is contraindicated in which condition?
w: Mannitol's large molecule stays in the blood and expands plasma volume, which can tip a failing heart. Urea is the one contraindicated in renal disease, and glycerol the one contraindicated in diabetes.

q: A Krukenberg spindle is pigment deposited on which corneal layer?
w: Three rings, three layers: Krukenberg spindle is pigment on the ENDOTHELIUM in pigment dispersion syndrome; the Fleischer ring is iron in the EPITHELIUM in keratoconus; the Kayser-Fleischer ring is copper in DESCEMET'S in Wilson's disease.
k: Krukenberg endothelium; Fleischer epithelium; KF Descemet's.

q: The Fleischer ring of keratoconus is iron deposited in which layer?
w: The Fleischer ring is an iron line in the corneal EPITHELIUM at the base of the cone. Do not confuse it with the Kayser-Fleischer ring, which is copper in Descemet's.

q: Anterior chamber depth changes with age from about 3.6 mm to:
w: The chamber shallows from about 3.6 mm to 3.0 mm as the lens thickens (+28% axial thickness by age 70). That shallowing is why angle-closure risk climbs with age.
k: AC depth 3.6 → 3.0 mm with age; lens +28% by 70.

q: Aqueous humor production declines by roughly how much per decade after age 30?
w: Production falls about 25% per decade after 30. IOP nevertheless tends to hold or rise, because outflow facility declines in parallel.

q: Retinal nerve fiber layer thinning over a normal lifetime amounts to about:
w: A normal eye loses 20-30% of its RNFL across a lifetime, which is why OCT must be read against age-matched norms rather than a fixed number.

q: CMV retinitis becomes a risk once the CD4 count falls below:
w: CMV retinitis is the classic opportunistic retinitis of advanced AIDS, appearing below a CD4 of 50, with its brushfire border and hemorrhage.

q: Giant cell arteritis is laboratory-supported by an ESR above:
w: An ESR above 50 supports GCA in a patient over 55 (mean age 70) with jaw claudication. Steroids start immediately on suspicion; biopsy follows.

q: A diabetic cataract can progress to maturity in as little as:
w: Osmotic swelling from sorbitol accumulation can mature a diabetic (snowflake) cataract within 72 hours, a speed no age-related cataract approaches.
k: Diabetic cataract can mature in 72 hours.

q: Shaken baby syndrome is essentially confined to children under:
w: Under 2 years the head is heavy relative to weak neck muscles and the vitreous is firmly adherent, so shaking produces the multi-layered retinal hemorrhages that define the syndrome.

q: A pigment imprint on the anterior lens capsule after blunt trauma is called a:
w: A Vossius ring is iris pigment stamped onto the anterior lens capsule when the iris is driven against the lens by blunt force. It is a marker of the impact, harmless in itself.
k: Vossius ring = iris pigment on anterior lens capsule.

q: Separation of the iris root after blunt trauma produces which pupil shape?
w: Iridodialysis tears the iris root free, giving a D-shaped pupil and a second 'pupil' at the periphery. A peaked pupil instead suggests an open globe with iris prolapse.

q: Commotio retinae typically resolves within:
w: Commotio retinae is grey retinal opacification from photoreceptor outer-segment disruption, and it clears in 2-6 weeks. At the macula it can mimic a cherry-red spot, but unlike CRAO it is self-limiting.

q: Angle recession after blunt trauma requires long-term monitoring because it risks:
w: A tear in the ciliary body face scars the outflow pathway, and glaucoma can appear years or decades later. This is why a documented angle recession earns lifelong IOP checks.
k: Angle recession = late glaucoma, monitor for years.

q: Retinal dialysis after blunt trauma occurs at the:
w: Retinal dialysis is a peripheral disinsertion at the ora serrata, commonest in young males after blunt trauma. It needs scleral indentation to see.
k: Retinal dialysis = disinsertion at the ora serrata.

q: A white crescent concentric with the optic disc after blunt trauma is a:
w: Choroidal rupture appears as a white crescent concentric with the disc, initially masked by subretinal hemorrhage. Sight depends on whether the rupture crosses the fovea.
k: Choroidal rupture = white crescent concentric with disc.

q: The best imaging for a suspected orbital blow-out fracture is CT with:
w: Coronal CT shows the orbital floor and any entrapped inferior rectus in cross-section; axial cuts run parallel to the floor and can miss it entirely.
k: Blow-out fracture: CT with CORONAL sections.

q: POAG and PACG differ in that PACG classically presents with:
o: Sudden pain with a fixed pupil
w: POAG is the silent thief: gradual, painless, peripheral loss first, treated medically. PACG is the emergency: sudden pain, halos, cloudy cornea, mid-dilated fixed pupil, ciliary injection, nausea, treated surgically by iridotomy.
